claims.claim: The stress-based early warning method identifies the transition point from exponential to linear growth in surface stress, enabling thermal runaway prediction approximately 2000 s in advance for 50% SOC batteries.
claims.quote: By monitoring transition point of stress variation, thermal runaway can be predicted up to approximately 2000 s in advance.

claims.claim: For 0% SOC batteries that do not undergo thermal runaway, the stress signal provides a warning approximately 816.3 s before the safety valve opens.
claims.quote: Even in cases where 0 % SOC battery does not undergo thermal runaway, the stress signal can provide a warning approximately 816.3 s before the safety valve opens, significantly enhancing evacuation and rescue time.

claims.claim: A stress change rate exceeding 0.45 kPa/s is defined as the threshold value indicating the starting point of the early warning for thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the stress change rates corresponding to the transition points of these two stages are both higher than 0.47 kPa/s. Therefore, considering the safety margin, 0.45 kPa/s can be regarded as the starting value for the warning.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during thermal runaway reaches 427.3°C for the 100% SOC battery, compared to 317.1°C for the 50% SOC battery, a difference of approximately 110°C.
claims.quote: during thermal runaway, the maximum temperature (TTR, max) reaches 427.3 ℃ for the 100 % SOC battery, compared to only 317.1 ℃ for the 50 % SOC battery. Notably, the maximum temperature for the 100 % SOC battery exceeds that for the 50 % SOC battery by nearly 110 ℃.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway trigger time for the 100% SOC battery occurs nearly 1200 s earlier than for the 50% SOC battery under the same overheating conditions.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway trigger time for the 100 % SOC is nearly 1200 s earlier than that for the 50 % SOC.

claims.claim: The peak mass loss rate during thermal runaway for the 100% SOC battery is 7.24 g/s, significantly higher than the 2.72 g/s observed for the 50% SOC battery.
claims.quote: the mass loss rate curve for the 50 % SOC battery peaks at 2.72 g/s during thermal runaway, significantly lower than the peak value of 7.24 g/s observed for the 100 % SOC battery.

claims.claim: The second peak stress during thermal runaway for the 50% SOC battery is 138.25 kPa, lower than the 224.88 kPa observed for the 100% SOC battery, attributed to slower chemical reactions at lower SOC.
claims.quote: the second peak stress for the 50 % SOC battery is 138.25 kPa, lower than the 224.88 kPa observed in the 100 % SOC battery. This difference is attributed to the slower chemical reaction in the 50 % SOC battery during thermal runaway compared to the 100 % SOC battery.

claims.claim: For 280 Ah LFP batteries, thermal runaway does not occur at 0% SOC even when the front surface temperature approaches 400°C, because most lithium ions have been transferred to the positive electrode, resulting in slower chemical reactions.
claims.quote: for the 0 % SOC battery, the temperature rise rate remains slow until Tf approaches 400 ℃, suggesting that thermal runaway does not occur... when the battery's SOC is at 0 %, most of the lithium ions in the negative electrode have been transferred to the positive electrode, leading to a slower chemical reaction rate and a gradual voltage drop, which prevents thermal runaway from occurring.

claims.claim: When the safety valve opens on 280 Ah LFP batteries, the battery temperature decreases to some extent due to the Joule-Thomson effect, as high-pressure gas expands through the valve and performs work on the external environment.
claims.quote: the temperature of the batteries at different SOCs decreases to some extent when the safety valve opens, which is attributed to the Joule-Thomson effect... As the gas expands through the valve to perform work on the external environment, the overall temperature decreases, leading to a reduction in the battery's temperature.

claims.claim: Traditional monitoring methods such as voltage drop and temperature surge provide warning only within 20 s before thermal runaway occurs, whereas gas detection via safety valve opening provides warning nearly 1000 s in advance.
claims.quote: traditional methods, such as monitoring voltage drop or temperature surge, are only effective within 20 s before thermal runaway occurs, posing a high risk. Detection of gas release can provide an early warning of thermal runaway nearly 1000 s in advance.

claims.claim: The reaction onset temperature for cathode-anode and anode-electrolyte reactions roughly advances (decreases) as SOC increases in NCM523/graphite 18650 batteries.
claims.quote: the reaction onset temperature for cathode-anode and anode-electrolyte roughly advances as the SOC increases

claims.claim: The reaction enthalpy of cathode-anode, anode-electrolyte, and cathode reactions increases with increasing SOC in NCM523/graphite 18650 batteries.
claims.quote: the reaction enthalpy of the cathode-anode, anode-electrolyte and cathode increases with the increase of SOC

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, anode-electrolyte and cathode-anode reactions account for 52.2% and 40.9% of total heat generation during thermal runaway, respectively, while cathode and separator account for only 5.6% and 1.2%.
claims.quote: the heat production from the anode-electrolyte sample and the anode-cathode sample are the main heat of the battery during TR, accounting for 52.2% and 40.9% respectively at 100% SOC, while heat productions from cathode and separator only account for 5.6% and 1.2% respectively

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, the maximum temperature rise rate of the NCM523/graphite 18650 battery is 12.564 °C/min, which does not meet the thermal runaway criterion, and therefore thermal runaway does not occur.
claims.quote: at 0% SOC, the maximum temperature rise rate of the battery is 12.564 °C/min, which does not meet the criteria for TR, and therefore there is no T5 for battery with 0% SOC

claims.claim: The battery safety valve opening temperature decreases as SOC increases, indicating that the rate of gas production within the battery increases with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: the battery safety valve opening temperature decreases as the SOC increases, indicating that the rate of gas production within the battery increases as the SOC increases

claims.claim: The abnormal expansion force can be detected at temperatures as low as 35.4°C during overcharging, which achieves an early warning signal 11 minutes earlier than the onset of battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The result shows that the abnormal expansion force can be detected at temperatures as low as 35.4 ◦C, which achieves an early warning signal 11 min earlier than the onset of battery thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The time interval between the warning signal of the expansion force and temperature increases steadily from 151 s to 682 s as the charging rate decreases from 6 C to 1 C.
claims.quote: the time interval between the warning signal of the expansion force and temperature increases steadily from 151 s to 682 s as the charging rate decreases.

claims.claim: The charging rate hardly affects the SOC boundary of venting, which remains at approximately ±118% SOC regardless of charging rate.
claims.quote: the charging rate hardly affects the stage of charge boundary of venting, which is around ±118 %.

claims.claim: At charging rates of 5 C and 6 C, the internal short-circuit temperature (TSC) decreased significantly to 101.2°C, compared to approximately 120°C at charging rates of 1–4 C, attributed to rapid proliferation of Li dendrites at high charging rates.
claims.quote: Based on the temperature at which the voltage drop rate reached the maximum (TSC), the TSC at CRs of 1–4 C was approximately 120 ◦C. However, at CRs of 5 C and 6 C, TSC decreased significantly and the SC temperature decreased to 101.2 ◦C. This phenomenon is attributable to the rapid proliferation of Li dendrites within batteries subjected to high CRs.

claims.claim: The maximum expansion-force rise rate ((dF/dt)Max) before safety valve venting increased from 31.4 N/s at 1 C to 505.6 N/s at 6 C, indicating a strong positive correlation between charging rate and maximum force rise rate.
claims.quote: (dF/dt)Max increased continuously with the CR. In particular, the effect of the CR on (dF/dt)Max was extremely significant, with (dF/dt)Max reaching 31.4 N/s and 505.6 N/s at CRs of 1 C and 6 C, respectively.

claims.claim: When the charging rate was lower than 3 C, a plateau stage was observed in the expansion-force rise rate during battery overcharging, but the plateau disappeared at charging rates higher than 3 C.
claims.quote: When the charging rate was lower than 3 C, there would be a plateau stage was observed for the expansion-force rise rate during battery overcharging, while the plateau disappeared with charge rate higher than 3 C, and the expansion-force rise rate continued to increase.

claims.claim: The SOC boundary at which the battery is completely filled with gas decreases as the charging rate increases during overcharging, specifically from 105% SOC at 1 C to 103% SOC at 6 C.
claims.quote: the SOC boundary at which the battery is completely filled with gas decreases as the CR increases during overcharging.

claims.claim: The efficacy of the expansion-force early warning method at the module and system levels requires further additional validation and cannot be directly extrapolated from cell-level results.
claims.quote: its efficacy at the module and system levels requires further additional validation. For instance, in configurations involving multiple rows of modules, it becomes necessary to equip each row with a force sensor.
